era: Olympia to Capitol Hill. Radio/Label. E=3 -- curatorial energy. Evergreen State College fanzine editor turned Sub Pop co-founder. The person who identified the Seattle sound. 1983-present.

location.origin: Olympia, Washington. The Evergreen State College.
location.formative: Olympia's DIY music scene. Evergreen State College. The Rocket (music newspaper). KCMU orbit. The Olympia-to-Seattle pipeline. K Records, Kill Rock Stars, and Sub Pop all rooted in Olympia.
location.career: Sub Pop Records. Capitol Hill, Seattle. Co-founded 1986 with Jonathan Poneman. The curatorial half of the partnership. Stepped back mid-1990s. Published Sub Pop USA (2014).

members.name: Bruce Pavitt
members.note: The curatorial half of the Sub Pop partnership. Fanzine editor, music writer, scene identifier. Evergreen State College. Olympia roots. The ear that heard the Seattle sound.

associated_musicians.name: Jonathan Poneman
associated_musicians.note: The business half. Poneman (business) + Pavitt (curatorial) = Sub Pop. Complementary halves. Consecutive degrees 127-128.

associated_musicians.name: Nasty Nes Rodriguez
associated_musicians.note: Hip-hop DJ. Part of the KCMU/radio trio: Nasty Nes (126), Poneman (127), Pavitt (128). Three consecutive radio/label figures.

associated_musicians.name: Calvin Johnson
associated_musicians.note: Olympia label founder. K Records provided the philosophical template for PNW independent labels. Pavitt emerged from the same Evergreen/Olympia ecosystem.

associated_musicians.name: Slim Moon
associated_musicians.note: Olympia label founder. KRS represents the middle of the Olympia label evolutionary sequence: K Records (pure independence) -> KRS (political independence) -> Sub Pop (pragmatic independence).

associated_musicians.name: Jack Endino
associated_musicians.note: The producer who defined the early Sub Pop sound: quick sessions, minimal overdubs, the sound of the room. Mudhoney, Nirvana (Bleach), TAD.

associated_musicians.name: Mudhoney
associated_musicians.note: Touch Me I'm Sick (1988). The definitive early Sub Pop band. Pavitt's curatorial ear in action.

associated_musicians.name: Nirvana
associated_musicians.note: Bleach (1989). The band that went from Sub Pop to global phenomenon. Pavitt signed them.

associated_musicians.name: Soundgarden
associated_musicians.note: Hunted Down (1988). Early Sub Pop single. Pavitt recognized the heavy, raw sound.

theory_nodes.concept: Sub Pop founding pair complete (127-128) -- Poneman (business) plus Pavitt (curatorial) equals Sub Pop Records; complementary halves that could not have built the label independently; the partnership that defined Seattle music
examples: Poneman (127): capital, business structure, promotional genius, Nirvana royalty deal, Warner partnership.
Pavitt (128): curatorial ear, subcultural credibility, national context from fanzine years, aesthetic vision.
The complementarity: one ear, one wallet, one label. Neither could have built Sub Pop alone.
theory_nodes.description: Sub Pop founding pair complete. Business (127) + curatorial (128). Complementary halves.

ancestors: Sub Pop label credits throughout the engine. The label has been present; now both founders are documented.
Poneman at degree 127 established the business half. Pavitt at 128 completes the pair.
genealogy.relationship: Founding pair complete at degree 128. Track Sub Pop curatorial decisions.

theory_nodes.concept: KCMU/radio trio complete (126-128) -- three consecutive radio/label figures from the KCMU orbit: Nasty Nes Rodriguez (126, E=4), Poneman (127, E=3), Pavitt (128, E=3); energy descent from performer to architects; one station, three cultural curators
examples: Nasty Nes (126, E=4): on-air DJ presence, hip-hop.
Poneman (127, E=3): business architect, label infrastructure.
Pavitt (128, E=3): curatorial visionary, fanzine-to-label pipeline.
KCMU as training ground for cultural curators across genres.
theory_nodes.description: KCMU/radio trio complete. Three consecutive degrees, three radio/label figures, one station.

ancestors: KCMU/KEXP as cultural institution. The station that played the bands nobody else would play.
The trio represents three facets of music infrastructure: performance (DJ), business (label exec), curation (A&R/writer).
genealogy.relationship: Radio trio complete at degree 128. The KCMU pattern is documented.

theory_nodes.concept: Olympia label trilogy (43, 124, 128) -- three labels with Olympia/Evergreen State roots: K Records (43, pure independence), Kill Rock Stars (124, political independence), Sub Pop (128, pragmatic independence); evolutionary sequence from one city
examples: K Records (43): Calvin Johnson. Beat Happening. Lo-fi as aesthetic choice. Anti-commercial.
Kill Rock Stars (124): Slim Moon. Bikini Kill, Sleater-Kinney. Riot grrrl distribution. Independence as political statement.
Sub Pop (128): Pavitt/Poneman. Nirvana, Mudhoney. Independence with business infrastructure and pragmatic survival.
Evergreen State College: the common origin. An experimental college producing label founders.
theory_nodes.description: Olympia label trilogy: K Records, Kill Rock Stars, Sub Pop. Three philosophies, one city.

ancestors: The Evergreen State College as cultural hothouse. Olympia's disproportionate influence on PNW music.
Each label represents a different approach to the same question: how can independent music sustain itself?
genealogy.relationship: Olympia trilogy complete at degree 128. Track Olympia connections.

theory_nodes.concept: Fanzine-to-label pipeline -- institutional evolution: column (1980) to fanzine to cassette compilation (1983) to record label (1986); the organic progression from critic to curator to institution builder; curation as the seed of infrastructure
examples: Pavitt's Sub Pop column in The Rocket (1980): music criticism as curation.
Sub Pop fanzine: photocopied, hand-stapled, covering the American underground.
Sub Pop #5 cassette compilation (1983): the transition from writing about music to presenting music.
Sub Pop Records (1986): the label as the logical conclusion of the curatorial impulse.
theory_nodes.description: The fanzine-to-label pipeline. Criticism becomes curation becomes institution.

ancestors: DIY publishing tradition: fanzines as cultural infrastructure.
The Olympia/Evergreen ethos: make the thing yourself, don't wait for permission.
genealogy.relationship: Fanzine-to-label pattern at degree 128. A model for institutional organic evolution.

cross_references.relationship: CO-FOUNDER PAIR COMPLETE! Poneman (business, 127) + Pavitt (curatorial, 128) = Sub Pop. Complementary halves. The pair is documented across two consecutive degrees.
KCMU TRIO COMPLETE! Nasty Nes (126, E=4), Poneman (127, E=3), Pavitt (128, E=3). Three consecutive radio/label figures from KCMU. Energy descent: performer to architects. Complete.
OLYMPIA LABELS! Kill Rock Stars (Olympia, 124) and Sub Pop (Olympia-origin, 128). Both rooted in Evergreen State/Olympia culture. Slim Moon (KRS) and Bruce Pavitt (Sub Pop): Olympia label founders.

OLYMPIA TRILOGY! K Records (43) -> Kill Rock Stars (124) -> Sub Pop (128). Three labels with Olympia roots. Evolutionary sequence: pure independence -> political independence -> pragmatic independence.
